13.1.label: Question 13.1
13.1.question: Explain what might happen if an insurer doesn’t use a consistent basis between related products.

Solution: The insurer will end up selling two fairly similar products, at the same time, of which one must be
expensive relative to the other. If the market is competitive, this might affect consumers’ buying
and discontinuance behaviour:
         more of the cheaper product might be sold relative to the other
         holders of the more expensive product may lapse their existing policies and take out new
cheaper ones, to the disadvantage of the insurer
         policyholders could feel aggrieved at the perceived inequity, with resulting bad publicity
for the insurer
         the insurer’s profits will become more sensitive to the type of business sold (ie its profits
will be at an increased risk from changes in the mix of new business).

13.2.label: Question 13.2
13.2.scenario:
You are reviewing the adequacy of premium rates for a range of contracts.

Discuss the extent to which margins against adverse contingencies are required in the following
cases. Where you think margins are required, state with reasons how and where in the basis the
margins might be applied.

Twenty‐year critical illness insurance: The extent of margins will depend on the degree to which premiums might be reviewable during
the policy term.                                                                           [½]
The more frequently they can be reviewed, the lower the margins need to be, provided there is no
restriction on the amount to which premiums can be increased.                                [½]
Such a restriction may result from marketing requirements, as an increase in premiums in excess
of what policyholders are prepared to pay will lead to selective lapsing and even worse
experience following the review.                                                              [½]
Margins are needed to cover poor claim experience because of:                                           [½]
         poor assumption‐setting as a result of scanty or inappropriate experience data                [½]
         disease‐diagnosis rates accelerating more quickly than expected, due to unforeseen
medical advances                                                                              [½]
         new diseases occurring which, perhaps due to loose claim definitions in the policy
wording, lead to more claims being made                                                       [½]
         a higher rate of disease incidence in the population than expected                            [½]
         a greater extent of selective withdrawal than expected                                        [½]
         a different mix of business sold than expected, where cross‐subsidies in the claim
experience have been allowed for – eg by distribution channel, policy size.                   [½]
The margin would take the form of an increase to the assumed claim incidence rate in the
basis.                                                                                                  [½]
A high lapse rate might be assumed early on when the earned asset share is negative, ...                     [½]
... and possibly lower lapse rates later in the term when a profit would be made on lapse.                   [½]
There is little point in assuming a margin in the interest rate assumption for regular premium
critical illness insurance because it would make little difference to the premium rate.        [½]
This is because the reserves are relatively small and the investment contribution to profit is small.
If the contract were single premium (unlikely), a low interest rate would guard against
reinvestment risk (although even here there will normally be little, if any, reinvestment.)                  [½]
Expenses could be higher than expected,:          the policy administration requiring more work than expected (this is particularly likely in
         fewer policies being issued than expected causing a greater per‐policy cost of fixed
         inflation of expenses being higher than expected.                                                  [½]
It would be sensible to include margins by increasing the expense assumption, ...                            [½]
... and increasing the inflation rate assumption.                                                       [½]

There is likely to be much uncertainty o:          the length of time the policyholder will live                                                      [½]
         the rate at which claimants will move into higher care categories (if this leads to increased
annuity payments under the contract).                                                      [½]
Assumed mortality rates therefore need to be reduced.                                                        [½]
Transition rates to higher care categories, where these exist, need to be increased.                         [½]
Depending on how the benefits are defined under the policy, it may be that these will be affected
by increases in long‐term care costs (eg nursing home fees), and may turn out higher than
expected.                                                                                      [½]
A higher inflation rate for benefit levels should then be assumed.                                           [½]
If policies are well matched by assets, then there will be a reduced need for a margin in the
interest rate assumption.                                                                                    [½]
However, the uncertainty over the duration (and possibly amount) of future claim payments
means that assets and liabilities may end up being significantly mismatched.                                 [½]
Also, investment return is a significant component of future cashflow and so it would then be
necessary to have a margin in this assumption.                                                [½]
A reduction in the assumed investment return would be made.                                          [½]
Future expenses could be higher than expected, as in (a). This is especially true if changes in
benefit levels are allowed under the contract when care needs change.                                [½]
Higher expenses, and expense inflation, should again be assumed.                               [½]
[Maximum 4]

Private medical insurance: These are one‐year contracts so they are effectively yearly renewable. Margins are therefore
needed less than in the other contracts, ...                                                 [½]
... because the renewal premiums can be revised in the event of worsening claim and expense
Margins will still be necessary, in order to make a profit.                                          [½]
Higher than expected claim incidence rates, expenses and average claim amounts could be used,
... or an explicit overall margin could be added.                                                    [½]
A particular explicit margin might be needed for the rate of renewals assumed when spreading
A lower than expected rate of renewal would be used.                                                 [½]
Some claims (particularly the more expensive ones) may result in payments that continue for
several years. In such cases, medical inflation will be an important influence on claims costs. [½]
A higher rate than expected should be assumed in order to provide the margin.                        [½]
Reserves will be small, and so the investment return will not be a significant factor. Therefore
little loading will be needed for this.                                                          [½]

13.3.label: Question 13.3
13.3.scenario:
A proprietary insurance company issues conventional long‐term health insurance products.

Describe the factors that the actuary of the company would take into account, and the modelling
investigations he or she would carry out, when considering the extent of margins that should be
incorporated into the basis:

(i).question: when pricing using a cashflow approach

Factors: Margins are needed when pricing, in order to cover the risk that future experience turns out to be
more adverse than assumed in the pricing basis.                                                 [½]
Ultimately, therefore, the extent of margins incorporated will be those deemed necessary to
protect the company from making unacceptable losses from the product.                                [½]
The following are the main factors to ta: 1.        The extent of uncertainty associated with each parameter, which will reflect the
credibility, accuracy and relevance of the data used to estimate them.                             [1]
As well as reflecting the data, parameter uncertainty is also increased by the nature of the
variables involved.                                                                       [½]
For example, claim rate experience can be significantly affected by the degree of
subjectivity involved in the claim process when interpreting claim definitions …                  [½]
… and several variables (ie claims, inflation, withdrawals, new business volume) can be
affected by social, economic and political influences, ...                              [½]
... which are very difficult to predict.                                                          [½]
The greater the uncertainty, the higher the margin needs to be.                                   [½]
2.        The extent of statistical (random fluctuations) risk inherent in the future experience.           [½]
This means the inherent / random, nature of the experience could lead to adverse
outcomes, even where the underlying expectations were estimated correctly.                        [½]
3.        The financial significance of the adverse experience that could result from (1) and (2)
above.                                                                                            [½]
The greater the financial significance of the adverse outcomes, the greater the margin
needed.                                                                                [½]
The financial significance will also reflect the extent of guarantees incorporated in the
product.                                                                                  [½]
For example, the extent to which premiums, charges, or other terms and conditions might
be reviewable can significantly reduce the financial onerousness of guarantees, ... [½]
... and so reduce the margins needed.                                                             [½]
4.        The company’s attitude to risk.                                                                   [½]
This ultimately reflects the shareholders’ attitudes: the more they are prepared to put
their capital at risk, the lower the margins that will be needed.                       [½]
5.        The proprietors’ required return on capital.                                                      [½]
The higher the required return, the higher the risk discount rate (and hence margin)
needs to be.                                                                                      [½]
6.        The size of the company’s free assets or the extent of protection provided by a parent
company.                                                                               [½]
Free assets and capital protection may mean that the company may be able to have lower
margins.                                                                            [½]
7.        The competitive nature of the market and the company’s unique selling proposition.                [½]
These influence how much a given size of margin will adversely affect business volumes
(ie including the impact on both sales and withdrawals).                              [½]
The level of margin needs to be a satisfactory compromise between the need to reduce
risk on the one hand, and the need to maintain sufficient business volume on the
other.                                                                              [½]
Investigations: The main idea is to investigate the financial impact of the possible levels of adverse experience, ...
... then to assess the margins that should be sufficient to cushion the company sufficiently.         [½]
The models will need to be profit‐tested, both on a single policy (model point) basis, ...            [½]
... and also for the office as a whole.                                                               [½]
Single policy profit test: This model will project the regular profits expected from a single representative policy, …            [½]
... and then to produce a single net present value (NPV) by discounting at the RDR.                   [½]
It is the addition, or margin, to the RDR that is being assessed.                                     [½]
One way of doing this would be to perform a deterministic sensitivity analysis.                       [½]
A possible approach to this would be to assume an initial premium level, perhaps based on a
Then by varying the future experience assumptions over the range of plausible outcomes
(eg those occurring with 95% probability), the impact on the return on capital can be calculated.
This in turn should indicate how much the required return (ie the RDR) needs to be increased in
order to compensate for the potential shortfalls.                                              [½]
Additionally it may be possible to run the model stochastically, modelling the random statistical
risk using the assumed probability distributions.                                               [½]
This would enable the variance of the return on capital to be assessed directly from the stochastic
output, from which the RDR margin can be assessed as before.                                    [½]
The method is limited in that it is not always possible to identify appropriate probability
distributions to use, at least not for all variables (eg lapse rates).                                [½]
A further alternative, again limited to certain variables, is to use analytical methods to assess the
impact of the variance of the particular variables on the variance of return.                       [½]
Model office: The impact of the new product on the company’s future solvency position and on the company’s
future embedded value can be investigated, ...                                            [½]
... by incorporating future sales of the new product within a full projection model of the
Further sensitivity tests can be performed with the full model, to assess the financial impact on
the company as a whole of the likely range of variation in experience.                            [½]
This may change the actuary’s view of the financial significance of particular assumptions and may
therefore lead to a different margins being chosen.                                             [½]

(ii).question: when establishing the level of supervisory reserve.

Solution:          The data are likely to be scarce, so that the graduation could be very unreliable and
inaccurate over large parts of the age range. Using a standard table, which is based on a
much larger body of data, gives the company more confidence that its assumption will be
realistic.
         The company has a greater chance of making a significant error if it is setting its own
assumptions without reference to a standard table.
It might be possible to do a straightforward sum of the individual illness incidence rates.
However, depending on the data, this might lead to ‘double counting’ of CI claims, for example, if
an individual was diagnosed with both cancer and heart disease, this would lead to a single CI
claim, but may be included twice if the individual rates are simply added together. In this case, an
adjustment would be needed.
It is very likely that claims incidence (or the pattern of diagnosis) could change differently over
time for different illnesses. For example, new screening techniques could accelerate times of
diagnosis for certain illnesses, increasing claim incidence rates for those causes but not for others.
There are many other factors – developments in medical science, specific changes to underwriting
practice, changes in policy wordings, etc – which can be specific to different illness types so
producing different effects on incidence rates for each disease.
Note that the adjustments could also be age specific, as changes in morbidity over time may not
be the same at every age.
Additionally, sophisticated IP insurance and PMI models may allow for higher incidence
rates among lives that have claimed historically and recovered – in effect, previous claims
experience is being used as a risk factor.
PMI will be most affected because the policies are all short‐term in nature, generally renewable
annually. Policyholders have to make the decision consciously to renew their policies at the
renewal date, so that a large hike in premiums could put off large numbers of policyholders from
renewing.
Long‐term products have guaranteed annual renewal until the expiry of the policy term.
Policyholders have to make a conscious decision to stop paying premiums under their existing
policies (ie to lapse their policies).
There may also be an incentive to keep a long‐term policy going (at least to its next review date in
the case of reviewable premiums), particularly if the company has increased its charging rates for
new business since the existing policy was taken out.
There is no population equivalent of lapsing a policy (members of a population can die or become
sick, but not ‘lapse’ from the population). So no data exist.
A company’s own set of lapse rates will be highly dependent on distribution methods and
after-sales service. Hence the use of statistics from another provider is at the very best a
short-term measure.
After‐sales service is a factor that mostly affects short‐term products, such as PMI, where there is
regular contact between insurer and policyholder at each annual renewal.
The results of such analyses should be assessed to see whether they have been affected by
special factors such as an adverse economic situation in the country.
The level of economic confidence and the ongoing competitiveness of the product sold are
two other key determinants.
The total value of the profit from the policy as at the date of surrender would be equal to the
asset share minus the surrender value.
If the surrender value exceeds the asset share, then the policy makes a loss equal to the surrender
value minus the asset share.
         Thirdly, at any time, the effect of lapses may be selective, such that the healthier
lives will be more likely to lapse, leaving a worsening propensity to claim among
those continuing.
For policies that pay indemnity benefits, the above will mean that the continuing policies
will generally be those that incur larger, as well as more frequent, claims.
For example, PMI lapse rates are also heavily correlated with claims experience –
generally the lowest claiming members have lapse rates that are significantly higher
than the rate of the highest claiming members, since the latter will find it more
difficult to obtain similar insurance cover with another insurer and are likely to value
the benefits of the product more highly.
Modelling should be employed to estimate the susceptibility of the product design and the
company’s capital to each form of lapse.
The idea is to get an indication of the sensitivity of such things as profit, price and capital
requirements to changes in the lapse rate assumption, so the insurer can understand how
important the assumption is and therefore how much care and effort it should apply in order to
determine the assumption. (This is called sensitivity testing).
Factors: When assessing margins for reserving bases, the following factors are additional and over‐riding
         ensure that the requirements of the relevant supervisory regulations are met                     [½]
         ensure that the margins achieve the necessary degree of prudence for a supervisory
The necessary level of prudence may be obtained through holding solvency capital or some other
form of risk margin rather than including margins in the assumptions.                       [1]
The factors mentioned for pricing that relate, ultimately, to commercial considerations (ie items
(4) to (7) in the earlier list of pricing factors) are of no relevance when it comes to setting
Investigations: Similar techniques to those used in pricing will be in assessing the margins required for reserving,
ie stochastic modelling, sensitivity analysis and analytical approaches may all be used.          [½]
The difference is that the impact on the present value of profits is now of no interest.                   [½]
Instead, the idea behind reserving is to ensure a large enough reserve is held (together with the
solvency capital) to cover the future net outgo from the existing (in‐force) business, with a
A cashflow model of the company’s existing in‐force business would be most appropriate for this
For example, by modelling claims experience stochastically, the increase in reserve that would be
necessary to cover future claim outgo with a very high (eg 97.5%) probability could be assessed.
Syllabus objective: 5.1     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for health and care insurance business.
5.1.1     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for pricing health and care
5.1.2     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for determining liabilities.
5.1.3     Explain why the assumptions used for supervisory reserves may be different
5.1.4     Outline the principles of setting assumptions for determining embedded
0 Introduction: This chapter covers the derivation of the demographic assumptions used in health and care
insurance. The focus here is on deriving assumptions for pricing, although deriving them for other
In an ideal world, the pricing actuaries would be able to decide for themselves on the most
appropriate assumptions to use. However, it can be the case that the regulatory authorities will
constrain this choice in a pricing context.
The values assigned to the parameters should reflect the expected future experience of the
lives who will take out the contract being priced.
Note the use of the word ‘reflect’ rather than ‘equal’. The expected future experience is just the
starting point in putting a value on a parameter, and it would then be necessary to consider what
The expected future experience of the po:          the target market for the contract – this will be dependent on the distribution channel
involved, and the territory (geographical region) chosen
         the underwriting controls applied at the start of the policy
 the claim controls applied at:          the policy wording, eg under a CI contract where the diseases covered are defined
         the expected change in the experience since the time of the last historical investigation to
the point in time at which the assumption will on average apply (eg as a result of medical
1.1 Data: In most cases, morbidity and mortality parameters will be based on an adjustment to rates,
or transition probabilities, from a standard table.
This very much depends on a suitable standard table being available. For example in the UK,
standard tables exist for CI and IP claim rates, but not for LTCI (because of insufficient data) or for
PMI (because the insurance companies are reluctant to disclose their data in sufficient detail).
The data would relate to an appropriate period of years, such that the volume of data is
adequate, but excessive heterogeneity due to changes over time is not introduced.
There is a conflict here between the desire to have a large pool of data, and a requirement not to
look at significantly different cohorts of lives. If the data include lives that enjoy different
morbidity due to coming from different generations, then the morbidity implied by the data may
be misleading. This will be so if differently aged lives have seen different morbidity improvements
from one generation to the next. So morbidity investigations are normally based on three or four
years of data.
If the company has insufficient data to produce reliable results, or has no appropriate data
at all, industry sources, such as the Continuous Mortality Investigation reports and working
papers produced in the UK, consultancies or reinsurance companies, might be used
instead, if appropriate for the product and parameter in question.
Such data might also be particularly useful for showing trends, since trends in an insurance
company’s own data might be down to statistical variation. Population morbidity experience
provides another important source of data for setting assumptions. All these sources of data
were discussed in Chapter 12.
1.2 Grouping the data: The analysis would divide the data into relevant homogeneous groups, subject to adequate
levels of data being retained within each cell.
A similar argument, of credibility versus heterogeneity, applies here. If the morbidity experience
of men and women were analysed when grouped together, then the morbidity rates observed
will reflect the proportions of males to females in each group. The grouped rates will not
represent future grouped rates if this proportion were to vary over time, as males and females
1.3 Calculating the rates: Adjustments to standard tables would typically be simple in form, for example an insurer might
define its future stand‐alone CI claim incidence rates for heart disease, as i xHD , to be:
where ixHD(S) is the claim incidence rate by heart disease at age x in a suitable standard table, and
a and b are chosen constants.
Using standard table rates in this way is an example of using market data – in particular insured
lives data – as a basis for setting assumption.
Question: Suggest reasons why an insurance company might prefer to use standard table rates in the above
formula, rather than using independently graduated rates from its own claims experience.
Why are the individual illness incidence rates projected forwards before they are combined?
Why will renewal rates under PMI policies be more affected by increases in inflation compared
with the other main health and care product types?
List the subdivisions of data that you might use in order to analyse the lapse rate experience of
own company data, for CI policies.
How can the overall profit (or loss) made by a policy that pays a surrender value on withdrawal be
calculated?
         When monitoring the experience, it is often convenient to compare your own experience
with a standard table, especially if this is a commonly used standard throughout the
industry. This would enable the company to see how its experience compares with the
industry as a whole, as well as indicating when (and how) its pricing basis may need
changing.
If the company has adequate data, the adjustment (to the standard table) would be derived
by analysing the company’s own experience for the type of contract concerned.
Alternatively, the experience of a similar class of business could be used as a substitute.
This alternative may provide appropriate data, depending on the similarity between the contracts
concerned. For example, suppose an insurance company wants to price a new IP policy with a
3‐month deferred period and the company has issued IP policies with a 6‐month deferred period for
many years. It might be possible to use the historical data from the 6‐month policies to provide a
basis for the 3‐month inception rates.
Using the experience of ‘similar’ contracts as a basis for assumptions also depends on the similarity
of the target markets involved. So, in the above case, the past experience of the 6‐month policy
data might not be fully relevant to the 3‐month policy design, if different kinds of people (eg in
different occupational groups) were to be attracted to the 3‐month version.
The more different the type of policy, the less similar will be the class of lives involved and the more
different will be their experience rates. For example, using CI claim incidence rates as a basis for IP
inception rates could be extremely problematic.
1.4 Adjustments: If the adjusted rates are to apply to a class of lives that is expected to have a different
experience from that to which the analysed data relates, then further adjustments may need
This situation could arise due to a change in target market, distribution channel, or the
basis of underwriting and accepting lives. It is worth noting however that, for many classes
of health and care risk, no pre-standardised data may exist and the insurer must gather less
relevant information from other sources and adapt this to its own risk profile (as described
Note that the class of lives concerned is almost always going to have a different experience from
that underlying the data, just due to the influence of time.
Claim incidence rates include:          rates of transition from healthy to claiming for IP policies
         rates of claim occurrence for CI policies, for each disease / procedure type separately
         rates of transition from able to claiming, and from a lower benefit level to a higher level,
         rates of claim occurrence for PMI policies, for each benefit category separately.
2.1 Data: We begin by choosing the most suitable database. In some instances (reinsurers’ data or
market data), this may come in the form of incidence rates. If not, the rates must be
2.2 Grouping the data: The rates will need to be broken down into discrete risk cells. After due analysis these may
then be combined.
A risk cell represents a collection of risks (lives) who all share the same attributes with regard to
the particular factors that are deemed to influence morbidity. So males aged 45, who smoke,
work in professional occupations and who live in the Salford area of Manchester could be a single
risk cell.
Analysing the data by the different factors that affect risk enables the insurer to offer different
terms (eg different premium rates) to people with different risk classifications. A major reason for
doing this is to reduce the risk of anti‐selection from high‐risk groups, and to increase the
attractiveness of the product to the lower‐risk groups. The subdivisions are necessary so that the
individual effects of each factor can be accurately assessed, and appropriate prices determined.
The big problem with analysing the experience of individual risk cells separately is that the volume
of data in each is so small that no credibility can be given to the results, and so no useful
information (about the effects of each factor) can be obtained. Not all risk factors are equal in
their relative effects on morbidity, so by combining risk groups whose morbidity is relatively
similar, the insurer can obtain significantly larger but still relatively homogeneous risk groups.
Trial and error combinations of risk cells followed by comparison of the experience rates between
them can help determine the most appropriate combinations to use ultimately for pricing
purposes. The insurer would then expect to end up with different sets of premium rates or
charges for different risk groups, eg according to age, gender, smoker status, and so on.
 size of benefit selected:          class of product (eg whether stand-alone, rider to savings vehicle)
 duration since policy entry:          duration since start of current benefit, where appropriate (eg for LTCI transition rates to
         underwriting status (ie whether the policyholder was accepted at standard rates or was
In some countries, differential pricing according to one or more of these factors may be
prohibited. However, internal company analysis may continue to monitor the experience
Following a ruling by the European Court of Justice (ECJ), insurance companies operating within
the EU may no longer charge different rates for men and women. A further concern is whether
age discrimination may also be disallowed by the ECJ in the future.
For PMI, market practice (or equality legislation) in some countries has ruled gender out as
a rating factor. The company may continue to analyse its experience by gender and
arguably the claims differential between the sexes can be very significant.
Smoking status is not yet widespread as a premium determinant in PMI and the size of
benefit is not relevant. Premium rates are generally split by age (sometimes age group).
2.3 Calculating the rates: Where the probability of claim is deemed to be a compound of many different events that
have different propensities, the incidence rate is derived from the combination of these
probabilities. This is the case for critical illness and possibly also PMI. Long-term care and
income protection incidences tend to be derived as single rates.
For PMI and other short‐term contracts, the future premium rates tend to be applicable for one
year at a time, at the end of which policies are up for renewal and different rates can be charged.
Projections of incidence rates for each benefit category are then much less problematic than for
the long‐term health insurance contracts. However, expected claim amounts for each benefit
class for the period in question are also required, and these will need projecting as well.
For CI, rates are derived separately for each of the main classes of claim, eg cancer, heart
attack, stroke, coronary artery bypass surgery, total permanent disability. The others are
derived individually or proportionately. Each is projected separately, before being
combined to produce the global incidence rate. However, this type of analysis may not be
suitable for tiered benefits or policies where multiple claims are possible.
So, incidence rates will be determined for each of the main critical illnesses, based on past data.
These will then need to be projected forwards so that they are best estimates of the future
experience. Finally, a single claim incidence rate is determined by combining the individual illness
2.4 Adjustments: Adjustments will have to be made, as discussed above, to produce rates that are relevant to
the company, its products and the timeframe for which they are to be sold.
Allowance will be made in all incidence rates for trends, both improving and worsening, so
that the premiums charged reflect the likely claims outgo in the appropriate period of risk.
Trends will be most relevant if they are derived from the insurance market under
Social and economic influences: There are several social and economic factors that influence the propensity of an individual
to claim under a health and care insurance product (or indeed to continue claiming income
benefits) and to continue paying premiums.
Of particular note in a health and care:          the future plans for State welfare provision (eg where PMI is an alternative or
         the economic wellbeing of the country (eg rising unemployment can affect IP
Government promises in welfare provision: The more generous the State promises in relation to long-term disability support and acute
medical care provision, the more difficult it will be to acquire and retain business.
Pessimism in the economy and the levels: This may encourage some employees to take advantage of group PMI cover whilst they are
still employed, hence increasing the PMI claims incidence rates.
Similar economic circumstances may provide a disincentive for some IP claimants to return
to work; this may be especially true of self-employed workers who may find that the number
The state of the economy and unemployment levels also influence lapse or renewal rates
High inflation: This will impact the renewal price of PMI and is likely to reduce renewal rates or result in
In addition, this will affect PMI claims experience because it will increase the risk of selective
lapsing. This will also be true of long‐term health and care product types if they have index‐linked
premiums, however, the effect will be most severely felt in the case of PMI.
3 Recovery rates: Recovery rates are important to any contract where the benefit is paid out in the form of an
         under LTCI, the benefit will cease if the policyholder recovers from disability or dies
– recovery is unlikely given the claim conditions and age profile of the beneficiary,
and hence is generally ignored in pricing
         under IP insurance, the benefit will cease if the claimant recovers or dies or if the
policy expires; the rate of recovery or rate of continuation is crucial to the costing
3.1 Data: A large database will be needed to be able to calculate rates that are age, gender and
duration dependent, and will allow for the influences of occupation and cause of claim.
3.2 Grouping the data: As for incidence rates, differential pricing might be prohibited in respect of one or more of
these factors, but the company may continue to split its data in this way for analysis.
 size of benefit:          class of policy (particularly the length of the deferred period)
 whether individual or group:          length of time since start of claim (or since start of sickness)
3.3 Adjustments: Medical advances will play a role in both aiding speedier recovery and extending life in
disability, where formerly death might have ended the claim.
This means that the effect of medical advances on total expected claim costs – and hence
morbidity claims experience overall – is not clear cut.
The motivation to return to work is a key factor in controlling claim continuation rates.
The economic environment (availability of work) may be outside the insurer’s control.
However, policy conditions (the ratio of all post-disability income, including IP insurance
benefit, to pre-disability salary) can be managed to ensure that experience is in line with
expectations.
Policy conditions can be used to ensure there is a financial incentive for the claimant to return to
work. These conditions take effect at the date of claim and would be set to limit the benefit to
some proportion (less than 1) of total income prior to the claim starting. The need is to ensure
that total income while claiming is below that which would be earned if the policyholder stopped
claiming.
Claims management and the involvement of the insurer in rehabilitation can also have a
significant impact on recovery rates.
Rehabilitation to help claimants to return to work as soon as possible is a key aspect of IP claims
management. Allowance for the effects of this should be taken into account when setting
recovery rate assumptions, both when interpreting historical data and when considering any
future adjustments that might be necessary.
Claims for large benefit amounts tend to continue for significantly longer than those for
small amounts.
4.1 Data: Mortality rates should be taken from the recent experience of a credible body of
Ongoing claims (eg under IP and LTCI contracts) may terminate for three reasons: recovery, death
and reaching the end of the term of the contract. Claim termination rates for given ages can
therefore be obtained by combining the appropriate recovery and mortality rates, bearing in mind
the termination ages of the contracts (if there is one).
 policies without a death benef: This then has an impact on how much effort would normally be expended on determining the
Non‐claiming vs claiming policyholders: The actuary will need separate rates of mortality, among healthy (non-claiming) lives for IP
insurance, LTCI and CI insurance, and among lives in claim for IP insurance and LTCI.
For stand‐alone CI, IP and LTCI, the non‐claimants’ mortality would merit the least research, and
the rates would probably be based on a standard mortality table – quite possibly for insured lives
– but with adjustments to allow for future mortality improvements. The same formulaic
approach as used for claim incidence rates, described in Sections 1.3 and 2.3, would probably be
For IP and LTCI claims in payment, more effort would be required in order to estimate the future
mortality rates. This is because the assumption has a bigger financial impact, and because the
mortality of sick lives is a lot less predictable than for the population as a whole. The subdivisions
of data used would probably be the same as for claim recovery rates.
Claims within the survival period: Special consideration needs to be given to the survivorship requirement for stand-alone CI
The CI ‘survivorship requirement’ is the policy condition that requires a policyholder to survive for
a specified period of time (eg 28 days) following the date of illness diagnosis, in order for a claim
It is usual for the effect of mortality during the survivorship period to be allowed for by an
adjustment to the claim incidence rate. The adjustments will be specific to the different illness
categories (as the post‐diagnosis survivorship can differ quite dramatically for the different
Policies without a death benefit: As a rate of decrement, mortality is deemed less significant than others that give rise to
For a stand‐alone CI contract, reducing mortality rates by 10% will increase the population
exposed to risk of critical illness by far less than 10%. (For example, if mortality rates are normally
expected to be 0.005 each year, then even if they reduce by 100% (to zero) the number exposed
to risk of CI incidence would only be about half a percent bigger than it would have been.) This
means that the resulting increase in CI claims will be much lower than, say, if CI claim incidence
On the other hand, claim costs will be much more sensitive to mortality improvements after
claims have started (ie for IP and LTCI policies in payment).
There are two main influences at work:          the difference between the total claim cost between those that do or do not die after
starting benefits may be very significant, whereas someone who dies in the non‐claiming
state may never have claimed anyway
         the mortality rates of claiming (ie sick) policyholders are much higher than for non‐
claiming (ie healthy) policyholders, so the absolute impact of, say, a 10% reduction in
Where policy conditions do not have a significant death benefit, it is important that neither
mortality rate be over-estimated, as the whole contract would then be under-costed.
In the case of CI policies, ‘neither mortality rate’ refers to the mortality of CI policyholders while
healthy, and to the mortality of potential claimants following the date of illness diagnosis.
Because death reduces the probability of sickness claims occurring in both cases, it is important
not to overstate the mortality rate assumptions, unless there is a significant death benefit.
5 Lapses: A lapse describes the situation where policyholders decide to stop paying contractual premiums
under one of the long‐term products, or fail to renew their contract under a short‐term product.
In both cases, the implication is that no reimbursement is paid to the policyholder, and the policy
simply terminates at that point, with no benefits payable.
Most health and care products do not pay a cash sum to the policyholder on policy withdrawal.
The possible exception is long‐term care insurance, especially where it has a savings element (as
may be the case under a unit‐linked design). In this case, policy withdrawal would usually be
referred to as a surrender (with the payment of a surrender value).
The lapse assumptions should reflect the expected future experience in respect of the
contracts that will be taken out.
Each of the health and care insurance product types may exhibit different lapse experience.
Certainly, the PMI renewal experience will reflect the rate of premium increase, the
perceived service delivery within any State-provided alternative (length of waiting lists etc)
and the current confidence in the economy (where PMI competes with State provision).
The common theme here is that all of these factors affect the ongoing desirability of having the
insurance – either compared to what is available without insurance, or compared to the
5.1 Data: Lapse rates will be based on an analysis of the company’s recent experience. Ideally, this
should relate to the contract being priced, but if no such experience exists or the available
data are inadequate, then the experience under any similar contracts would be analysed.
If the company does not have adequate own data, there may be industry-wide experience
Market data sources are altogether much less useful for estimating lapse rate experience than is
the case for other demographic assumptions.
The reasons why market data are less use:          lapse rates are generally much more unpredictable than other demographic experience
rates because they are dependent on human choice (which may be influenced by economic,
political and commercial factors as well as general awareness)
         there is much more heterogeneity in lapse rate experience between insurance companies,
reflecting differences in distribution channel, target market, product design, benefits
provided etc, so the data will be less relevant
         past data and future experience are both influenced by changes to the economic
environment, the level of market competition and the standards of customer service; these
are all very difficult to allow for and to predict.
Reinsurers may also be able to provide lapse rate data, however, there will be no population data
that can be relevant to lapse experience.
5.2 Grouping the data: Own product lapse experience should be calculated as early as possible, split by
 policy type (eg stand alone, a:          different ‘series’ (eg issued in different past years with different policy wordings)
5.3 Adjustments: Determining a lapse rate assumption follows very much the same pattern as for the other
However, the big difference compared with other demographic assumptions is the big influence
of human behaviour on the future experience. While mortality and morbidity are generally things
that happen to policyholders against their wishes, stopping paying premiums is something that
So it is important to think about the factors that influence the policyholders’ decisions to lapse.
 political (eg what State benefits a:     commercial (eg whether there is a better or cheaper competitor’s product)
    awareness (eg whether there is a belief that the policy is necessary).
Adjustments are likely to be required to ensure that the assumptions derived from these
analyses reflect the expected experience of the lives being rated.
Adjustments should be made to allow for differences in characteristics that might affect lapse
experience between the data used in the analysis and that expected on future new policies..
Further details of analysing persistency experience are covered in Chapter 29.
Lapses affect the contract in several wa:          Firstly, an early lapse may cause financial loss, where insufficient premiums have
been received to recover the initial strain (commission and initial expense) involved
If the accumulated value of the physical cashflows (initial premium minus initial expenses
(including commission)) to the date of lapse is negative, then the insurer makes an overall
         Secondly, where no surrender value is payable and a reserve has been accumulated,
a lapse will work to the benefit of the insurer.
By ‘reserve’ here, the Core Reading is referring to the accumulated asset share, not the
supervisory reserve. So the only difference between this and the previous case is that, if a
lapse occurs when the asset share is positive, then the insurer makes a total
(accumulated) profit from the policy equal to its asset share at the date of lapse.
5.5 Lapse and re‐entry: Where competitive pressures drive down premiums, lapse and re-entry may be a particular
problem for insurers, especially if policies have large benefits.
Lapse and re‐entry is where policyholders find they would be better off lapsing their current
policies and taking out new ones.
This will be a problem when premium rates are falling, so that even though lapsing policyholders
are older than when their premiums were originally calculated, the premium rate for a new policy
at the current age is still cheaper.
For example, premiums on CI products in the UK fell during the 1990s as a result of competitive
The problem with this is that only healthy lives can benefit from lapse and re‐entry, as unhealthy
lives will not be able to take out new policies, or at least not at the standard rates of premium. So
the insurer will have the same total set of lives in force as it would have had without lapse and
re‐entry, but for a healthy sub‐group, a lower premium is being paid than before. In addition, the
insurer will incur another round of set‐up expenses and commissions, including underwriting
costs, for these lives. This will make the overall profitability from the portfolio lower than if lapse
and re‐entry had not occurred, and the portfolio could quite easily now be loss making.

The process of determining demographic a:          collecting appropriate data – the data would relate to an appropriate period of
years, such that the volume of data is adequate, but excessive heterogeneity due to
changes over time is not introduced
         grouping the data – the data would be divided into relevant homogeneous groups,
subject to adequate levels of data being retained within each cell; some cells may be
         calculating the rates – in some cases these will be based on an adjustment to a
standard table; in others exposed to risk techniques may be used
         adjusting the rates – if the adjusted rates are to apply to a class of lives that is
expected to have a different experience from that to which the analysed data
relates, then adjustments may need to be made.

Claim incidence and recovery rates: The insurer’s own data is likely to be the most relevant source of data. Reinsurers’ data,
market data and population data may also be useful.
Data will be grouped into risk cells. Legislation may prevent the use of certain rating factors
Allowance should be made for trends, both improving and worsening. Key social and
Claim incidence rates are very important for all product types. Single claim incidence rates
will be derived for IP and LTCI. Separate rates will be calculated for different illnesses for CI
insurance and for different treatments for PMI.
Claim recovery rates are important for IP insurance. Keenness to return to work, claims
management / the involvement of the insurer in rehabilitation and claim size are key
Medical advances should be carefully allowed for, as they can produce opposing effects on
overall claim costs, depending on cause of claim and product type.
Mortality: The process of deriving mortality assumptions is similar to that of deriving claim incidence

Lapse rates: The insurer’s own experience is likely to be the main source of data. Market and reinsurers’
data may also be used, however population data will not exist.
Lapse rates are more important when:          they occur early in the policy term, when a financial loss would be made
